m_Item1.QuestionText: A sort which relatively passes through a list to exchange the first element with any element less than it and then repeats with another element to exchange in list with any element greater than it is called____________
m_Item1.Explanation: Quick sort is based on the divide-and-conquer approach based on the idea of choosing one element as a pivot element and partitioning the array around it such that: Left side of pivot contains all the elements that are less than the pivot element Right side contains all elements greater than the pivot

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following sorting algorithms dones not have a worst case running time of O(n^2)?
m_Item1.Explanation: In the worst case analysis, we calculate upper bound on running time of an algorithm. We must know the case that causes maximum number of operations to be executed.  so answer is marge sort

m_Item1.QuestionText: The quick sort algorithm exploit _________ design technique
m_Item1.Explanation: Quick sort is based on partitioning of array of data into smaller arrays. A large array is partitioned into two arrays one of which holds values smaller than the specified value, say pivot, based on which the partition is made and another array holds values greater than the pivot value so work with divide and  Conquer design.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The  worst case time complexity of merge sort algorithm is
m_Item1.Explanation:
Merge Sort is a stable sort which means that the same element in an array maintain their original position with respect to each other.

Oveall time complexity of Merge Sort is O(n log n). It is more effient as it is in worst cose also the runtime is O(n log n).

m_Item1.QuestionText: The worst case occur in quick sort when

Options.OptionText: Pivot is the median of the array
Pivot is the smallest or largest element

m_Item1.Explanation: The worst case time complexity of a typical implementation of  The worst case occurs when the picked pivot is always an extreme (smallest or largest) element. This happens when input array is sorted or reverse sorted and either first or last element is picked as pivot.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The best average behaviour is shown by
m_Item1.Explanation: The key process in quickSort is partition(). Target of partitions is, given an array and an element x of array as pivot, put x at its correct position in sorted array and put all smaller elements (smaller than x) before x, and put all greater elements (greater than x) after x. All this should be done in linear time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following sorting algorithm is unstable?
m_Item1.Explanation:
A sorting algorithm is said to be stable if two objects with equal keys appear in the same order in the sorted output as they appear in the unsorted input.

Whereas a sorting algorithm is said to be unstable if there are two or more objects with equal keys which don’t appear in same order before and after sorting.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following sorting algorithms not have a worst case running time of O(n^2)?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Insertion sort: 
𝑂(𝑛2)
Merge sort: 
𝑂(𝑛log𝑛)
Quick sort: 
𝑂(𝑛2)(in the worst case, though on average it's 𝑂(𝑛log𝑛))
Bubble sort:
𝑂(𝑛2)
Merge sort consistently has a worst-case time complexity of 
𝑂(𝑛log𝑛)
O(nlogn), making it the correct answer.

m_Item1.Explanation:
In Quick sort, if the first element is chosen as the pivot element for partitioning. And Quick sort's  complexity - O(n^2).
Quick sort's worst case complexity of O(n^2) is observed when the input to be sorted is in decreasing order or increasing order (if the first elemnet is the pivot element).

m_Item1.QuestionText: The total number of comparisons required to merge 4 sorted files containing 15, 3, 9 and 8 records respectively into a single sorted file is ________
m_Item1.Explanation: We first merge 15 and 3 and get a list of 18 using 17 worst case comparisons. Then we merge 9 and 8 into a list of 17 using 16 worst case comparisons. comparisons. Finally we merge17 and 16  So total number of comparisons is 17 + 16 which is 33

m_Item1.Explanation: Quick sort  is also called as partition exchange sort.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A sort which relatively passes through a list to exchange the first element with any element less than it and then repeats with a new first element is called
m_Item1.Explanation: In quick sort every first element is compared with remaining elements & get swapped if it is smaller, & again repeats it, so on.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Given the following list of numbers [21, 1, 26, 45, 29, 28, 2, 9, 16, 49, 39, 27, 43, 34, 46, 40] which answer
illustrates the list to be sorted after 3 recursive calls to mergesort?
m_Item1.Explanation:
Total number of elements are =16
So array after first recursive call is = (0+15)/2 =7  array elements from 0 index to 7 index
[21, 1, 26, 45, 29, 28, 2, 9]
array after Second recursive call is = (0+7)/2 =3 array elements from 0 to 3
[21,1,26,45]
array after third recursive call is = (0+3)/2 =1 array elements from 0 to 1
[21,1]

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements explains insertion sort?

Options.OptionText: The list is broken apart into smaller lists that are sorted and merged back together.

The list is iterated through multiple times until it finds the desired first number, then repeats the
process for all numbers.

It removes one element from the list, finds where it should be located, and inserts it in that position
until no more elements remain.

All numbers less than the average are inserted on the left, and the rest is inserted at the right. The
process is then repeated for the left and right side until all numbers are sorted.

m_Item1.Explanation:
Insertion sort works similar to the sorting of playing cards in hands. It is assumed that the first card is already sorted in the card game, and then we select an unsorted card. If the selected unsorted card is greater than the first card, it will be placed at the right side; otherwise, it will be placed at the left side. Similarly, all unsorted cards are taken and put in their exact place.

The same approach is applied in insertion sort. The idea behind the insertion sort is that first take one element, iterate it through the sorted array. Although it is simple to use, it is not appropriate for large data sets as the time complexity of insertion sort in the average case and worst case is O(n2)

m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider an array of elements arr[5]= {5,4,3,2,1} , what are the steps of insertions done while doing
insertion sort in the array.
m_Item1.Explanation:
//Insertion sort logic
   for i = 1 to size-1 {
      temp = list[i];
      j = i-1;
      while ((temp < list[j]) && (j > 0)) {
         list[j] = list[j-1];
         j = j - 1;
      }
      list[j] = temp;
   }
